articles.title: Dashboard and Map — AtlasDays Help Center
articles.headline: Dashboard and Map
articles.description: Troubleshoot AtlasDays dashboard totals and map output: selected period, Days Abroad, US states, Transit, ongoing trips, and when the record needs fixing.
articles.intro: Check what the dashboard is summarizing, what the selected period changes, and why totals or map highlights can look wrong.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Helps You Do
section_contexts.summary: Use this page when totals, map highlights, or period filters do not match what you expected, and you need to work out whether the issue is in the dashboard or in the trip record behind it.
detail_points: what the selected Year , All Time , or Range actually changes
why Days Abroad , countries, trips, and tracker cards do not all answer the same question
why US states appear only when trips have state tags and you are using the full-screen map
when the right fix is in the trip record or settings instead of on the dashboard

section_contexts.heading: When the Dashboard Looks Wrong
section_contexts.summary: Most dashboard confusion comes from a small number of causes. Check these first before assuming the math is broken:
detail_points: Selected period: the current Year , All Time , or Range changes every stat, the map, the detail sheets, and the share cards.
Current trip record: the dashboard summarizes the trip record you have now. It is not reading from a separate hidden data layer.
Transit: Transit can still appear on the map or in country details, but it does not count as a visited stay and adds 0 days .
ongoing trips: an ongoing trip uses today as its effective end date, so totals keep moving until you close it.
Same-day crossings: one date can legitimately belong to two countries if you left one and entered another on the same day. That can make countries and trips move in a way that feels surprising.

section_contexts.heading: What the Dashboard Is For
section_contexts.summary: The dashboard turns your current trip record into a period-based summary. It helps you answer questions like how many countries you visited in a period, how many unique days you were abroad, and how that record looks on the map.
detail_points: The dashboard summarizes the record you already have. If the number looks wrong, first check the selected period and the trip entries behind it.

section_contexts.heading: The Main Dashboard Stats
section_contexts.summary: The four stat tiles at the top are tappable, and each one opens the trips or country rows behind the number.
detail_points: Countries: how many distinct visited countries fall inside the selected period. The denominator depends on your visited-country counting mode in Settings: UN 193 , UN 195 , UN 195+2 , or All Places . Transit -only countries appear separately on the map, but they do not count as visited countries. Tap Countries to open the Countries sheet, where you can also reveal and sort unvisited places.

Trips: how many non- Transit trips fall inside the selected period. All Time can include trips stored as Year or Unknown . A specific Year can include matching Year entries. Range is the strictest view and focuses on trips that can be placed inside that exact window.

Days Abroad: total unique exact days outside your Home Country in the selected period. If you chose No fixed residence , all recorded days from Exact Dates trips count as abroad. This is not the same thing as tracker days, because trackers use their own country scope, time window, and optional night counting.
Continents: how many continents are represented by the visited countries in the selected period. If countries drop out because of the current period or counting mode, continents can change too.

section_contexts.heading: Country Count and Continent Settings
section_contexts.summary: Country Count changes the visited-country denominator and which places count toward the country total. It does not rewrite your trips or change tracker math.
detail_points: Settings also lets you choose how transcontinental countries count in continent breakdowns: their primary continent, their secondary continent, or both. That affects the dashboard continent list and continent share card, but it does not change trip dates, visited-country totals, or tracker results.
UN 193 counts UN member states only.
UN 195 adds the two UN observer states.
UN 195+2 adds Kosovo and Taiwan.
All Places includes tracked territories and dependencies as separate places.

section_contexts.heading: Trackers on the Dashboard
section_contexts.summary: If you have trackers set up, they appear as cards below the stats. Each card shows the tracker name, current total, threshold, and progress state.
detail_points: Those cards use the same underlying trip record, but not the same question as Days Abroad . A tracker can use a different country scope, a rolling or stay-based window, and either day or night counting, so its number does not need to match the dashboard total.
You can reorder tracker cards by dragging, and collapse or expand the whole tracker section. If the tracker logic itself looks wrong, use Trackers and Limits .

section_contexts.heading: Changing the Period
section_contexts.summary: The selected period changes what appears everywhere on the dashboard. When you switch periods, you are not changing one tile. You are changing the whole summary.
detail_points: Year mode lets you pick a specific year or All Time . Range mode lets you choose a window like the last 7, 30, 90, 180, or 365 days, or set a manual start and end date. Use Year when the question is about a calendar year. Use Range when the question is about a rolling or custom window.
Approximate trips do not behave the same way in every period:
All Time can show trips saved as Year or Unknown .
A specific Year can show matching Year entries.
Range is best for Exact Dates analysis. A Year entry appears there only when the selected range fully covers that year. Unknown entries do not land inside a specific Year or Range .

section_contexts.heading: Using the Map
section_contexts.summary: The dashboard shows a mini world map. Tapping it opens the full-screen interactive map where you can tap any country to inspect the trips behind that highlight.
detail_points: The map reflects the selected period and the same trip record behind the stats. It is a visualization of that record, not a separate calculation layer.

Visited countries are highlighted in your chosen accent color. Transit places appear differently so you can tell them apart from entered-country visits. Very small countries like Vatican City, Singapore, and Malta can appear as dot markers because their territory is too small to see reliably at map scale.

section_contexts.heading: How Day Counting Works
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays counts both the arrival day and the departure day. A trip from January 1 to January 3 is 3 days , not 2.
detail_points: Shared dates from Exact Dates trips are not double-counted in unique dashboard day totals. If you have overlapping trips, or a legitimate same-day border crossing, one calendar date still counts once toward Days Abroad . An ongoing trip uses today as the effective end date until you close it.

That is why some results can feel unintuitive while still being correct. Two same-day border crossings can increase Trips or the country list, while Days Abroad only moves by one unique day for that date.

section_contexts.heading: When to Fix the Record Instead
section_contexts.summary: The right fix is in the record or settings, not on the dashboard, when any of these are true:
detail_points: If one of those applies, clean up the trip record first. The dashboard will update from that record.
a trip is saved as Year or Unknown when you actually know the Exact Dates
Transit is marked incorrectly
there are duplicate or overlapping entries
an ongoing trip should already be closed

section_contexts.heading: Share Cards
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays can generate shareable cards from your dashboard data, including a map snapshot and your key stats. You can swipe through a carousel of card styles and toggle each card between light and dark appearance independently from your system theme. Save to Photos or share directly.
detail_points: The cards are rendered from your actual trip data for the selected period. If the card looks wrong, the thing to verify is still the selected period and the trip record behind the dashboard.

section_contexts.heading: Where to go next
section_contexts.summary: Trip Modes and Record Quality explains how Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , Transit , duplicates, overlaps, and ongoing trips affect the record behind the dashboard.
detail_points: Trackers and Limits explains why a tracker total can differ from Days Abroad or other dashboard summaries.
AtlasDays Pro explains what is free and what unlocks US state tracking.

images.prompt:
Create a 3:2 website illustration for atlasdays.app.
This image supports product documentation for AtlasDays, a private iPhone app for trip history, visa days, and residency tracking. It should feel product-adjacent without recreating a literal app screenshot, and should still carry some travel-product personality.
Use a premium travel-tech editorial illustration style that fits AtlasDays: dark charcoal and cool slate as the base, deep oceanic navy depth, cyan-blue route accents, pale ice-blue surfaces, and small restrained warm highlights for contrast. The image should feel premium, modern, mobile-first, and travel-oriented: calm but not sterile, precise but not academic, aspirational without becoming generic travel-advertising fluff. Favor layered depth, subtle glow, motion, route energy, and destination atmosphere over flat icon-pack graphics. Do not make it whimsical, photoreal stock-photo-like, or cartoonish.
Topic concept: US visitor stays, entry and exit timing, and stay-limit planning.
Supporting context: Troubleshoot AtlasDays dashboard totals and map output: selected period, Days Abroad, US states, Transit, ongoing trips, and when the record needs fixing.
This artwork will appear beneath an existing webpage title and subtitle, so the image itself must not contain any heading, subtitle, sentence, or label. Minimal threshold numerals are acceptable, but avoid poster-style copy or UI-like text.
Do not make the image look like a poster, slide, infographic header, or book cover.
Use a dark neutral background that separates cleanly from both the light and dark website themes. Blue should appear as an accent, not as a full flat background.
Visual focus: US visitor stays, entry and exit timing, and stay-limit planning.
Helpful motifs: US outline, arrival and departure markers, count chips, calendar bars.
The image should feel like it belongs to a product people are excited to use for travel, not just a neutral reference article.
Bring in some sense of journey, movement, place, or destination atmosphere while staying clean and legible on the page.
When the image uses paper, cards, tables, or document-like surfaces, tint them with pale ice-blue, mist blue, or cool blue-gray rather than stark pure white so they integrate better with light mode.
Avoid large flat white blocks. Keep highlights soft, slightly cool, and premium.
Keep the image subtly connected to AtlasDays by using travel-tracking-adjacent motifs such as route lines, day-count chips, tidy cards, world highlights, or private-device context, but do not show a literal app screenshot.
Avoid readable text, letters, words, labels, titles, map labels, typographic elements, UI screenshots, government seals, watermarks, stock-photo cliches, passport-stamp collages, fake official forms, brand logos, or cluttered layouts. Avoid stiff textbook-diagram energy, generic corporate SaaS art, or dry flat iconography. If a counting concept is needed, represent it abstractly with bars, chips, rings, windows, or markers. Minimal numerals are acceptable only when they function as an abstract threshold symbol rather than readable UI text or copy.
Intro context: "Check what the dashboard is summarizing, what the selected period changes, and why totals or map highlights can look wrong."
Additional global art direction: Keep the images calm, premium, and clear on a documentation-heavy website. Favor strong silhouettes, clean geometry, and a travel-compliance tone over busy scenes or stock-photo energy.
Slot role: top-of-article hero image.
Composition goal: Create the main article cover image with one dominant subject and two or three supporting elements. It should feel like a proper top-of-page hero image that can sit under the page title.
Differentiate clearly from these sibling slots: body-1: interactive map detail

articles.title: Getting Started — AtlasDays Help Center
articles.headline: Getting Started
articles.description: Avoid early AtlasDays setup mistakes: choose Home Country or No fixed residence correctly, enter trips that affect live counts first, and know when trackers or import should wait.
articles.intro: Set up AtlasDays in an order that keeps Days Abroad , early dashboard totals, and first trackers from drifting off immediately.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Helps You Do
section_contexts.summary: Start here when you are setting up AtlasDays for the first time, restarting after skipping onboarding, or cleaning up an early setup before you rely on the numbers.
detail_points: Use the app in this order: Timeline holds the trip record, Dashboard shows what that record produces, and Settings controls Home Country , Import , iCloud sync , and permissions.
a clear Home Country decision, or No fixed residence if that fits you better
the trips that still affect live counts entered with the right precision first
a clear decision on whether to add a first tracker now, later, or only provisionally
a clear decision on whether Photo Import , CSV Import , or Flighty Import is worth doing yet

section_contexts.heading: Avoid These Setup Mistakes First
section_contexts.summary: Most early confusion comes from a few avoidable setup choices. Check these before you do anything else:
detail_points: Wrong Home Country: if Days Abroad looks wrong early, verify Home Country before you question the dashboard.
Wrong base model: choose a real Home Country when one country should be treated as home. Choose No fixed residence only when you do not want one country treated as home at all.
Approximate history before live trips: do not start by filling vague older trips if you still need to enter the trips that affect a live limit, residency threshold, or near-term paperwork question.
Trackers too early: a tracker can exist before the record is ready, but its result should be treated as provisional until the relevant trips are good enough.
Import before review: do not save a batch from Photo Import , CSV Import , or Flighty Import before checking which trips should be Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , or Transit .

section_contexts.heading: Set Up AtlasDays in This Order
section_contexts.summary: This order front-loads the decisions that change Days Abroad , live tracker math, and early dashboard trust. The app still works if you skip ahead, but early numbers are much easier to understand if you do these in sequence.

section_contexts.heading: 1. Decide your Home Country first
section_contexts.summary: Open Settings and set Home Country to the country that should be treated as home in the app. This is one of the first things to verify if Days Abroad or early dashboard totals look wrong.
detail_points: Choose a real Home Country if one country should be treated as home for abroad-vs-home questions. Choose No fixed residence if you do not want one country treated as home at all. In No fixed residence mode, Days Abroad counts all recorded days from Exact Dates trips instead of subtracting time in one home country.

Do this before entering lots of trips. If you add history first and change this decision later, Days Abroad can shift in ways that feel like bad math even when the app is doing exactly what you asked.

section_contexts.heading: 2. Add the trips that affect live counts
section_contexts.summary: Open Timeline and tap + . Start with the trips that can still affect a current visa limit, residency threshold, or near-term paperwork question. If a question is live now, that trip belongs early in the record.
detail_points: If the live question is about an individual US state, tag the trip with that state when you add a United States trip. Plain United States trips still count for country-level US questions, but they do not feed a state-specific tracker until they have a state. Saving US state tags requires AtlasDays Pro .
This step comes before trackers for a reason. A good tracker needs a good trip record. If you are close to a limit or threshold, slow down here and get these trips right first.
Use Exact Dates when the trip needs to feed real day counts, tracker math, or near-term paperwork.
Leave the end date empty only for an intentionally ongoing trip with Exact Dates . That trip keeps affecting totals until you close it.
Mark a trip as Transit only if you did not actually enter the country. Transit stays in history but counts 0 days .

section_contexts.heading: 3. Add older approximate history only after the live trips are right
section_contexts.summary: Use Year when you know which year a trip belongs in but not the exact dates. Use Unknown if you know you visited a place but cannot place it confidently in time yet.
detail_points: These entries preserve your record, but they do not create precise day totals or tracker counts. Year entries also cannot be future-dated.
Do not let rough older history delay the trips that still matter now. It is usually better to get the live-count trips correct first, then come back and fill older background history carefully.

section_contexts.heading: 4. Add your first tracker only when the relevant trips are ready
section_contexts.summary: Open Dashboard . If you do not already have a tracker, tap Add Tracker and choose the preset that matches the limit or goal you actually want to monitor.
detail_points: A tracker is useful early only if the trips behind that question are already good enough. If those trips are still approximate, either wait to add the tracker or treat its result as provisional until the record is cleaned up.

If onboarding already created a Residence tracker from your Home Country choice, that is normal. Review the trips behind it before acting on the number. For preset types, windows, alerts, and tracker trust, use Trackers and Limits .

section_contexts.heading: 5. Use import only when it is actually worth it
section_contexts.summary: Open Settings > Import when you have enough history that manual entry would be slow. Choose Photos when geotagged photo metadata is your strongest source. Choose CSV when your history already exists in a spreadsheet or travel notes. Choose Flighty when your best structured source is a Flighty flight-history export.
detail_points: Photo Import scans on-device metadata and drafts trips for review. Photo Import is free for any scan period you select. AtlasDays Pro is only needed here to preserve detected United States history as individual state-tagged trips.

CSV Import is worth it for a structured backlog. Flighty Import is worth it when flights cover most of the history you want to rebuild, but it still misses ground crossings and flights missing from Flighty. Manual entry is often cleaner for a small number of recent or important trips, especially when those trips affect live counts right away.

section_contexts.heading: When the Setup Is Trustworthy Enough
section_contexts.summary: You do not need a perfect lifetime travel history before you can trust a near-term question. You do need the trips behind that question to be right enough.
detail_points: Home Country or No fixed residence matches the way you want Days Abroad interpreted.
The trips that still affect live limits, residency thresholds, or paperwork are entered first, using Exact Dates when you can defend them.
Any ongoing trip is intentionally still open.
Transit is used only for non-entry travel.
Your first tracker is either based on ready trips or clearly treated as provisional.

section_contexts.heading: What Affects Counts and Results
section_contexts.summary: Home Country versus No fixed residence changes what Days Abroad means.
detail_points: Exact Dates feed tracker math and precise dashboard day totals.
Year and Unknown preserve history, but they do not generate exact counted days.
Transit stays in the record and may appear on the map, but it counts 0 days .
ongoing trips with Exact Dates use today as the effective end until you close them.
Overlapping Exact Dates trips can change totals and should be cleaned up before you rely on them.

section_contexts.heading: Where to go next
section_contexts.summary: Timeline and Calendar explains List and Calendar mode, inline suggestions, Find Photos, and overlap cleanup.
detail_points: Trip Modes and Record Quality is the next page if the real problem is trip precision, duplicates, overlaps, or deciding between Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , and Transit .
Trackers and Limits explains when a first tracker is worth adding, which setup choices change the result, and when a tracker should still be treated as provisional.

Avoid stiff textbook-diagram energy, generic corporate SaaS art, or dry flat iconography. If a counting concept is needed, represent it abstractly with bars, chips, rings, windows, or markers. Minimal numerals are acceptable only when they function as an abstract threshold symbol rather than readable UI text or copy.
Intro context: "Set up AtlasDays in an order that keeps Days Abroad , early dashboard totals, and first trackers from drifting off immediately."
Additional global art direction: Keep the images calm, premium, and clear on a documentation-heavy website. Favor strong silhouettes, clean geometry, and a travel-compliance tone over busy scenes or stock-photo energy.

Section summary: "Open Timeline and tap + . Start with the trips that can still affect a current visa limit, residency threshold, or near-term paperwork question. If a question is live now, that trip belongs early in the record."
Section detail cues: If the live question is about an individual US state, tag the trip with that state when you add a United States trip. Plain United States trips… | This step comes before trackers for a reason. A good tracker needs a good trip record. articles.title: Trip Modes and Record Quality — AtlasDays Help Center
articles.headline: Trip Modes and Record Quality
articles.description: Troubleshoot messy trip history in AtlasDays: choose Exact Dates, Year, Unknown, Transit, and ongoing correctly, and review Photo, CSV, or Flighty import before trusting counts.
articles.intro: Choose the right trip precision, clean up messy history, and know what to fix before you trust totals, trackers, or exports.
articles.plan_notes: Make the contrast between exact dates and year-only or unknown trips visually obvious without relying on readable UI text.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Helps You Do
section_contexts.summary: Use this page when you are adding trips manually, cleaning up old history, or trying to work out why dashboard totals, trackers, or exports look wrong downstream.
detail_points: By the end of this page, you should know:

If a trip affects a live visa limit, residency threshold, or near-term paperwork question, keep it in Exact Dates. Use Year or Unknown to preserve older history without making the precise counts less trustworthy.
when to use Exact Dates , Year , or Unknown
how Transit and ongoing trips affect the record
how duplicates, overlaps, and same-day crossings should be interpreted

section_contexts.heading: When the Trip Record Looks Wrong
section_contexts.summary: Most record problems come from a small number of choices or conflicts. Check these before you trust anything downstream:
detail_points: Wrong precision: a trip that should be Exact Dates may have been saved as Year or Unknown , which preserves history but does not support precise counts.
ongoing trip: an ongoing trip keeps affecting totals because AtlasDays uses today as the effective end date until you close it.
Transit marked incorrectly: Transit counts as 0 days . If you actually entered the country, the trip should not be marked as Transit.
Duplicate Exact Dates trip: this repeats the same stay twice.
Overlap conflict: this means different records share dates and need review.

section_contexts.heading: Exact Dates
section_contexts.summary: Use Exact Dates when the trip needs to feed real day counts, tracker math, or an upcoming application or compliance check. This is the mode to use for current trips, recent trips, and anything you can verify from stamps, tickets, or records.
detail_points: Exact Dates trips are the only trips that generate precise day totals.
They are also the only trips that can be ongoing or upcoming .
AtlasDays counts both the arrival day and the departure day for Exact Dates trips.
Use this mode for current trips, recent trips, same-day border crossings you can verify, and anything that affects live counts.

section_contexts.heading: Year
section_contexts.summary: Use Year when you know which year a trip belongs in, but you do not know the exact entry and exit dates. This is for reconstructed history, not for active counting.
detail_points: A Year trip can be a single year or a multi-year range.
Use it for approximate past history, not planned travel.
It preserves the visit without pretending you know the exact dates, and it can appear as visited history without contributing exact counted days.
Use it when the year is defensible but the exact dates are not.

section_contexts.heading: Unknown
section_contexts.summary: Use Unknown when you know you visited a place, but you cannot confidently place the trip in a year yet. In AtlasDays this is stored as a trip with no date fields.
detail_points: Unknown trips appear in the No Date section of the timeline.
They are useful for preserving history while you keep researching the dates.
They do not generate exact day counts or precise tracker results.
Use this only when even the year is still uncertain.

section_contexts.heading: Use Transit Only for Non-Entry Travel
section_contexts.summary: Mark a trip as Transit when you did not actually enter the country, such as an airside layover or similar pass-through travel. Transit entries stay in your record, but AtlasDays counts them as 0 days .
detail_points: If the trip should count toward time spent in the country, do not mark it as Transit even if it was short.

section_contexts.heading: How ongoing trips work
section_contexts.summary: Only Exact Dates trips can be ongoing . To create one, save the trip with a start date and leave the end date empty.
detail_points: They appear in the Ongoing section of the timeline.
AtlasDays uses today as the effective end date until you close the trip.
If you need a fixed total, close the trip before you rely on downstream counts.
If you need a planned future trip, use Exact Dates as well.

section_contexts.heading: Duplicates, Overlaps, and Same-Day Crossings
section_contexts.summary: Duplicates and overlaps are not the same problem, and a same-day crossing is not automatically either of them.
detail_points: For US state tracking, the state tag is part of the place. Two same-date United States trips in different states can be legitimate separate entries, while a plain United States trip may need cleanup if it overlaps later state-tagged rows. Review those conflicts before relying on a US State tracker.
Duplicate Exact Dates trip: the same stay was entered twice.
Overlap: two different records share dates and create a conflict that should be reviewed.

Same-day border crossing: if you departed one country and arrived in another on the same date, that shared date can be legitimate because AtlasDays counts both arrival and departure days for Exact Dates .
Not every shared date is suspicious: review the chronology before assuming every overlap is bad data.

section_contexts.heading: When the Record Is Trustworthy Enough
section_contexts.summary: You can start trusting downstream math when the record is precise enough for the question you are asking.
detail_points: Trips that affect live counts, trackers, or paperwork are saved as Exact Dates .
ongoing trips are closed if you need a fixed result.
Transit is used only for non-entry travel.
Duplicate Exact Dates trips and overlapping conflicts have been reviewed.
Same-day crossings have been checked and left as-is only when they reflect real travel.

section_contexts.heading: When to Use Import Instead of Manual Entry
section_contexts.summary: Use manual entry when you are logging a small number of trips, cleaning up recent travel, or still deciding whether an entry should be Exact Dates , Year , or Unknown .
detail_points: Use Photo Import when geotagged photo metadata is your best source for reconstructing old travel. Use CSV Import when you already have a spreadsheet, a large backlog of travel history, or rough source material you want to convert into a structured batch import. Use Flighty Import when Flighty already has the flights you want to turn into trips.
After an import is saved, use Settings > Manage Imports to inspect its batch or remove the whole batch if the source was wrong. Use individual trip editing when only a few rows need correction.
Decide or review the right precision for each trip first. Import does not resolve ambiguity for you.
Imported duplicates or overlaps should still be reviewed before you trust totals.
US state tags should be reviewed when a state-level tracker, map view, or export matters.

section_contexts.heading: How Record Quality Affects the Rest of the App
section_contexts.summary: Dashboard summaries reflect the trip record you have now, including Year , Unknown , Transit , and any open ongoing trip. Exact day totals still depend on Exact Dates .
detail_points: Trackers depend most on trips that are saved as Exact Dates . Year and Unknown preserve history but do not power exact tracker math.
US State trackers depend on United States trips being tagged with the specific state. Plain United States trips do not tell AtlasDays which state to count.
Export output reflects the same trip record and does not repair conflicts or bad precision choices for you.

Auto-Detect Trips surfaces new-place suggestions inline in Timeline. New trips still need review and confirmation, but later detection can automatically close an existing confirmed Open-ended trip when AtlasDays has evidence that you departed.

section_contexts.heading: Where to go next
section_contexts.summary: Timeline and Calendar explains how to browse, display, and clean up the records described here.
detail_points: CSV Import explains the file format, preview step, duplicate handling, overlap warnings, and the AI prompt helper.
Photo Import explains on-device photo metadata scanning, review, duplicate handling, overlaps, transit detection, and Photos privacy.

articles.title: Privacy, Location, and Sync — AtlasDays Help Center
articles.headline: Privacy, Location, and Sync
articles.description: Troubleshoot Auto-Detect Trips, US state detection, Photos access, Flighty or CSV import, notification permission, and iCloud sync expectations.
articles.intro: Troubleshoot Auto-Detect Trips, notification permission, and iCloud sync confusion without losing sight of AtlasDays' local-first privacy model.
articles.plan_notes: Focus on local-device privacy and optional sync. Do not use surveillance or creepy location-tracking imagery.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Helps You Do
section_contexts.summary: Use this page when Auto-Detect Trips is delayed or missing, notifications or tracker alerts do not arrive, or you want a clear answer about what stays local and what iCloud sync changes.
detail_points: By the end of this page, you should know:
AtlasDays is local-first. There is no AtlasDays account, no sign-in, and no AtlasDays server copy of your trip history. Trips, trackers, and day calculations work on-device by default.
why Auto-Detect Trips or alerts may not behave as expected
how ignored countries affect Auto-Detect Trip suggestions
what Photos access is used for during Photo Import

section_contexts.heading: When Auto-Detect Trips or Notifications Do Not Behave as Expected
section_contexts.summary: Most confusion here comes from how background detection and local alerts work on iOS. Check these first:
detail_points: Auto-Detect Trips is optional. AtlasDays still works fully for manual entry if you leave it off.
Auto-Detect Trips uses iOS significant location changes, not continuous GPS, so suggestions can be delayed and some crossings can be missed.
AtlasDays does not silently create a new trip. New detections become suggestions that you review, edit, confirm, or dismiss in Timeline.
If you already confirmed an Open-ended trip, later detection of a different place can close that existing trip on the detected departure date. This is an end-date update, not a newly created trip.
If the app is force-quit , background detection may stop until AtlasDays is opened again.

section_contexts.heading: What AtlasDays Stores on Your Device
section_contexts.summary: Your trip record and tracker data live in the app's local data store on your device by default. Manual trip entry, dashboard review, tracker calculations, Photo Import, CSV import, Flighty import, and export all work without an AtlasDays account.
detail_points: AtlasDays also keeps some device-level app state locally, such as pending trip suggestions, so the app can continue where you left off.
If you are signed into iCloud, AtlasDays can also mirror a smaller set of preferences through Apple's iCloud key-value storage. That preference carry-over is separate from full iCloud sync .

section_contexts.heading: How Auto-Detect Trips Works
section_contexts.summary: Auto-Detect Trips is optional. When you turn it on, AtlasDays uses iOS significant-change location monitoring to notice that you may have entered a different country and then resolves the country on device using bundled country boundaries. For Pro users in the United States, AtlasDays can also resolve the US state on device so a suggestion can be state-specific.
detail_points: AtlasDays does not silently create new trips from location events. A new place becomes a suggestion shown inline in Timeline, where you can open, edit, confirm, or ignore it. If AtlasDays is in the background and notification permission is allowed, you can receive a local notification.

There is one narrower automatic update: if you already saved an Open-ended trip, a later confirmed detection in a different place can close that existing trip on the departure date. AtlasDays can show an undo action when this happens in the app. If a same-day or next-day return proves that the departure was only a short excursion, AtlasDays can reopen a trip that it closed itself. A date you set manually is not reopened by this protection.
AtlasDays uses significant-change location, not continuous GPS tracking.
Because it is not continuous GPS, suggestions can arrive later than the actual crossing and some crossings may be missed.
Raw location history is not stored by AtlasDays and is not sent to an AtlasDays server.

section_contexts.heading: What Photo Import Does with Photos Access
section_contexts.summary: Photo Import is separate from Auto-Detect Trips . It scans geotagged photo metadata on device to draft past trips for review.
detail_points: Full Photos access is needed so AtlasDays can scan the library automatically.
AtlasDays reads metadata such as dates and locations. It can use altitude and media-type metadata to ignore likely airplane-window photos, screenshots, and screen recordings.
Videos can contribute dated location evidence, but they are not shown in the photo preview gallery.

AtlasDays does not copy your photo files into its own database, analyze image contents, or upload your photos to an AtlasDays-operated server. Imported trips can show thumbnails or open related photos from your Photos library.
The scan works without an internet connection and resolves countries with bundled boundaries on device.

section_contexts.heading: What CSV and Flighty Import Need
section_contexts.summary: CSV Import and Flighty Import use files you choose. They do not need Photos access, location permission, an AtlasDays account, or an AtlasDays server lookup.
detail_points: CSV files are parsed on device and can include an optional State column for United States trips.
Flighty CSV exports are parsed on device with bundled airport and airline references. Supported US airports can resolve to individual states.
Nothing from a CSV or Flighty file becomes a trip until you review and confirm the import.

section_contexts.heading: Which Permissions Matter
section_contexts.summary: If iOS location permission or notification permission is denied later, AtlasDays mirrors that and turns the related in-app toggle off rather than pretending the feature is still active.
detail_points: Location permission matters only for Auto-Detect Trips . Manual trip entry, dashboard review, import, and export do not require it.
Photos access matters only for Photo Import . Manual entry, CSV import, Flighty import, dashboard review, trackers, export, and Auto-Detect Trips do not require it.
Notification permission matters for background trip-suggestion notifications and tracker alerts. It is not required for manual logging.
If location permission or notification permission is denied at the iOS level, the related feature cannot behave normally.

For the most reliable background country detection, allow the location access level iOS offers for ongoing background use. If iOS offers an Always Allow upgrade, that is the setting AtlasDays needs for the most reliable background detection.

section_contexts.heading: What Notifications Are For and Why They May Not Arrive
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays uses local notifications for two jobs:
detail_points: These alerts are generated and scheduled on your device. They are not routed through an AtlasDays server.
Trip suggestions when the app detects that you may have entered a new country while AtlasDays is in the background
Tracker alerts , including Smart Alerts , when a tracker reaches its relevant alert state
If the app is open, you may see a trip suggestion in-app instead of depending on a background notification.
If notification permission is off, neither background trip-suggestion notifications nor tracker alerts can arrive normally.

section_contexts.heading: When to Use iCloud sync and When to Leave It Off
section_contexts.summary: Turn on iCloud sync if you want your trips and trackers available across multiple Apple devices or you want your Apple iCloud account to hold the sync-backed copy of that data.
detail_points: Leave it off if you use one device only, prefer to keep the full trip record local to that device, or would rather rely on manual file exports for backup and transfer.
This page covers the high-level choice only. For exact sync behavior, restart expectations, reinstall flow, and new-device details, use iCloud Sync and Restore .

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Does Not Solve
section_contexts.summary: If the real issue is reinstall, new-device setup, restart behavior, or exact sync expectations, use iCloud Sync and Restore .
detail_points: If the real issue is tracker thresholds, Smart Alerts , or whether a tracker is configured correctly, use Trackers and Limits .
If the real issue is the trip record itself, including whether an entry should be Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , or Transit , use Trip Modes and Record Quality .

section_contexts.heading: Where to go next
section_contexts.summary: Timeline and Calendar shows where Auto-Detect suggestions appear and how to review them.
detail_points: iCloud Sync and Restore explains what syncs, what does not, and what to expect after restart, reinstall, or a device change.
Trip Modes and Record Quality explains how Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , Transit , and ongoing trips affect the record behind alerts and trackers.

images.anchor_heading: How Auto-Detect Trips Works
images.context_summary: Auto-Detect Trips is optional. When you turn it on, AtlasDays uses iOS significant-change location monitoring to notice that you may have entered a different country and then resolves the country on device using bundled country boundaries. For Pro users in the United States, AtlasDays can also resolve the US state on device so a suggestion can be state-specific.

articles.title: Trackers and Limits — AtlasDays Help Center
articles.headline: Trackers and Limits
articles.description: Troubleshoot AtlasDays trackers: check country or US state scope, time window, trip precision, Smart Alerts, and when a tracker result is safe to trust.
articles.intro: Troubleshoot a tracker that looks wrong, keep the setup aligned with your rule, and verify when the result is safe to trust.
articles.plan_notes: The image should make rolling windows, yearly thresholds, and per-stay counting feel structured and understandable.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Helps You Do
section_contexts.summary: Use this page when a tracker looks wrong, when you are not sure the setup matches the rule you care about, or when you want to know whether the result is safe enough to act on.
detail_points: By the end of this page, you should know:
A tracker is a counting rule, not the record itself. AtlasDays derives it from your trip record, so incomplete, approximate, or unresolved trips will flow straight into the result.
why a tracker can look wrong even when the trip record seems close
which setup choices change the count and which do not
what to verify before you trust the result or expect Smart Alerts

section_contexts.heading: When a Tracker Looks Wrong
section_contexts.summary: Most tracker mismatches come from a small number of setup or trip-record issues. Check these before you change anything else:
detail_points: Country scope: confirm the tracker is pointed at the right country or preset set. A one-country tracker and a Schengen tracker can legitimately show different numbers for the same trip record.
US state scope: for a US State tracker, confirm the relevant United States trips are tagged with that state. Plain United States trips do not feed a state-specific tracker.
Window: confirm whether the question is Rolling , Per Stay , or Yearly . Those windows answer different questions.

Mode: confirm whether the tracker should be Stay under or Reach target . The same exact days can look very different depending on whether AtlasDays is showing remaining days or progress toward a goal.
Days vs nights: confirm whether the tracker is counting days or nights . From Dashboard , long-press the tracker card and choose Count Nights or Count Days for that tracker.

section_contexts.heading: When to Add a Tracker
section_contexts.summary: Add a tracker when you already know the question you want AtlasDays to answer, such as “How many Schengen days have I used?”, “How many days have I spent in this country this year?”, or “How long has this stay been since it started?”
detail_points: Add the tracker from Dashboard . If you do not have any trackers yet, Dashboard shows an Add Tracker entry point.
Add the tracker early if you want the structure in place, but do not rely on it until the relevant trips are exact and cleaned up.
If you are still rebuilding history, fix the trip record first in Trip Modes and Record Quality .

section_contexts.heading: What Trackers Do in AtlasDays
section_contexts.summary: Each tracker applies one counting rule to a selected country set. The same trip can affect multiple trackers at once, because each tracker can use a different country scope, threshold, direction, and time window.
detail_points: a preset set such as Schengen
a continent or region
any custom multi-country set you select yourself
individual US states (Pro)

section_contexts.heading: Residence
section_contexts.summary: Use Residence when you want to accumulate time in one country across a year-style period. It starts as a Reach target yearly tracker with a default threshold of 183 days , and you can still change the threshold, year, or fiscal-year-style period later.

section_contexts.heading: Schengen 90/180
section_contexts.summary: Use Schengen 90/180 when you want one preconfigured rolling upper-limit tracker for the Schengen area instead of building that country set manually.

section_contexts.heading: Visa Limit
section_contexts.summary: Use Visa Limit when the question is about one stay anchored to a clear start date. It starts as a Stay under per-stay tracker and is the closest fit for permit-based or single-stay limits.

section_contexts.heading: Travel Goal
section_contexts.summary: Use Travel Goal when you want to accumulate days toward a personal target rather than stay under a limit.

section_contexts.heading: US State
section_contexts.summary: Use US State when you want to track days spent in one or more US states. AtlasDays includes presets for commonly tracked states, each with caveats relevant to that state's rules, such as any-part-of-day counting for New York or domicile requirements. You can also choose a custom state set. Browsing presets is free; saving a US State tracker requires AtlasDays Pro .
detail_points: A US State tracker only counts trips tagged with that state. If your record has plain United States trips, edit them manually or use state-aware Photo, CSV, or Flighty import so AtlasDays knows which state should count.

section_contexts.heading: Custom
section_contexts.summary: Use Custom when the preset is close but not right. You can choose your own country scope, threshold, mode, and time window.

section_contexts.heading: Choose the Tracker Mode
section_contexts.summary: Stay under is for limits. AtlasDays shows days remaining, at limit, or over limit.
detail_points: Reach target is for goals. AtlasDays shows progress and how many more days you still need.
If the result feels conceptually backwards, check the mode before you change the trip record.

section_contexts.heading: Rolling
section_contexts.summary: Use Rolling when the question is “How many days have I used in the last N days?” The window moves forward every day, so old days fall off automatically. If you expected a calendar-year or stay-based total, this is the wrong window.

section_contexts.heading: Per Stay
section_contexts.summary: Use Per Stay when the question is about one stay rather than a whole year or rolling history. In AtlasDays, upper-limit per-stay trackers are anchored by a Tracking from date, so the stay has a clear start inside the app. If that date is off, the tracker will be off.
detail_points: This works best when the stay you care about is represented cleanly in the trip record. If that stay is split across messy or approximate entries, fix the trips first.

section_contexts.heading: Yearly
section_contexts.summary: Use Yearly when the question is “How many days in this year-style period?” If the total looks wrong, first confirm that the stored year or fiscal-year-style period is the one you intended. AtlasDays supports:
detail_points: the current calendar year
a specific stored year
a custom fiscal-year-style period

section_contexts.heading: Days or Nights
section_contexts.summary: Trackers can count either days or nights . If the number looks off by one, check this before you edit the trip record.
detail_points: Days are dual-inclusive. A trip from January 1 to January 3 counts as 3 days .
Nights are midnight crossings. The same trip counts as 2 nights .
From Dashboard , long-press the tracker card to switch that tracker between Count Nights and Count Days .

section_contexts.heading: What Counts Toward Tracker Math
section_contexts.summary: Why the country breakdown can look higher than the tracker total: in a multi-country tracker such as Schengen, the detail view can show the same calendar date under more than one country if you were present in multiple countries on that date. Each country row gets that day in its own subtotal, but the tracker total still counts that date once across the whole tracked area. If you were in three countries on one day, the country subtotals can add up to 3 days while the tracker total adds only 1 day .
detail_points: Exact Dates trips inside the tracker’s selected country set count, as long as they are not marked Transit .
US State trackers count the trip's state tag, not merely the United States country code.
Arrival and departure days both count for Exact Dates trips.
Shared dates from Exact Dates trips are counted once in the tracker total, not once per country row inside the tracker detail view.
ongoing trips use today as the effective end date, so the number changes day by day until you close the trip.

section_contexts.heading: What Does Not Count Toward Tracker Math
section_contexts.summary: Year trips stay in your history but do not feed exact tracker day totals.
detail_points: Unknown trips stay in your history but do not feed exact tracker day totals.
Transit trips count as 0 days .
Home Country does not change tracker math. It affects Days Abroad, not trackers.
Visited-country counting elsewhere in the app does not change tracker math. It only affects visited-country totals.

section_contexts.heading: When a Tracker Is Trustworthy
section_contexts.summary: You can act on a tracker when both the rule and the trips behind it match the real-world question. Use this checklist before you rely on the number:
detail_points: Treat the number as provisional if any relevant trip is still Year, Unknown, incorrectly marked Transit, or still ongoing when you expected a fixed total. AtlasDays will still count what it can, but you should not use that result as final until the trips behind it are exact and resolved.
the tracker is pointed at the right country set
the tracker is using the right mode : Stay under for a limit or Reach target for a goal
the tracker is using the right window : Rolling , Per Stay , or Yearly
the tracker is counting the right unit: days or nights

section_contexts.heading: Smart Alerts and Multiple Trackers
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays supports multiple concurrent trackers. Dashboard shows one tracker card if you only have one, and a swipeable set when you have several.
detail_points: For upper-limit trackers, AtlasDays starts with sensible warning thresholds such as 7 days remaining , 1 day remaining , and at limit where they apply. For target trackers, it can alert when the target is reached . You can adjust tracker alert thresholds and alert time in the tracker alert settings, and alerts are recalculated when your trip data changes.
Free includes one tracker.
AtlasDays Pro unlocks unlimited trackers.
Smart Alerts are an AtlasDays Pro feature and require notification permission.

section_contexts.heading: Why Smart Alerts May Not Fire
section_contexts.summary: Missing Smart Alerts do not prove the tracker is wrong, and delivered Smart Alerts do not prove the tracker is configured correctly. Review the tracker itself before you rely on it.
detail_points: AtlasDays Pro: Smart Alerts are not available on the free plan.
Notification permission: AtlasDays needs notification permission on that device.
Tracker setup: the tracker still needs the right country set, mode, and window, and the relevant trips need to be present in the record.
No matching alert point yet: do not expect Smart Alerts until the tracker reaches one of its configured alert states for its mode.

section_contexts.heading: Reading the Tracker Detail Chart
section_contexts.summary: Tracker Detail can show different chart shapes depending on the tracker's window. Rolling trackers show a rolling count over time. Yearly trackers show the calendar or fiscal-year period for the stored tracker year. Per Stay trackers show a cumulative chart when the tracker has a Tracking from date.
detail_points: Solid line: the day count produced by your trip record, day by day, up to today.

Dashed line: the projected count for days after today, based on ongoing and planned exact-date trips where the tracker can place them. This is not a saved forecast. It updates whenever you add, edit, or close a trip.

Threshold line: the tracker's limit or target, drawn horizontally across the chart. A floating label pinned to the right edge of the viewport shows the threshold value, and fades out while you scroll or scrub so it does not sit on top of the data.
Occupancy strip: the row of short bars above the x-axis marks the days you were present in the tracker's country set. Use it to see at a glance which days fed into the count.

Scrubbing: touch and drag on the chart to pin a cursor to a specific date. AtlasDays shows the exact day count on that date, the date itself, and a status label such as under limit , at limit , or over limit .

section_contexts.heading: Stay and Recovery Insights
section_contexts.summary: Below the chart, AtlasDays shows short forward-looking text derived from the same trip record the tracker uses:
detail_points: If a relevant future trip is still Year or Unknown , or ongoing with no end date, the projection cannot reflect it precisely. Fix the trip record rather than the chart.

The chart reflects your current trip record, not a promise. It assumes planned future trips happen on the dates you saved and can only be as precise as the trip dates behind it. If your plans change, edit the trip and the chart will update on its own.

When you will be back under the limit: shown when the current count is already over the threshold and you have a planned departure. AtlasDays simulates the rolling window forward to the first date where old days will have fallen out.

How an ongoing or planned trip affects your count: shown when you have an ongoing trip or planned future trips in the tracker's country set. Use this to see the effect of a scheduled trip before you take it.

section_contexts.heading: Adding a Trip From Tracker Detail
section_contexts.summary: Tracker Detail has a + button that opens the trip form pre-scoped to the tracker's country set. A one-country tracker locks the country field. A multi-country tracker shows a filtered picker so you can only pick from the tracker's countries. Use this when the chart or the trips section on Tracker Detail shows that a trip is missing and you want to add it without leaving the view.

section_contexts.heading: How Trackers Relate to Dashboard and Exports
section_contexts.summary: If you want to check the raw trip output before trusting a tracker, use Dashboard and Map to inspect the live record and Export and Reports to inspect the exported trip set.
detail_points: Dashboard tracker cards show the live tracker state from the tracker’s own rules and window.
Tracker Detail lets you inspect the trips and per-country totals currently affecting the tracker.

Do not assume the country rows should add up exactly to the tracker total. If the same calendar date appears in more than one tracked trip or country row, the tracker total still counts that shared date once.
CSV export exports trips, not tracker definitions.
PDF export summarizes trips and countries in the selected export period; it does not export tracker definitions.

section_contexts.heading: Where to go next
section_contexts.summary: Trip Modes and Record Quality explains how Exact Dates , Year , Unknown , Transit , and ongoing trips change tracker accuracy.
detail_points: Dashboard and Map shows how to compare the tracker card with the trips and totals currently driving it.
CSV Import and Flighty Import explain how imported United States history can preserve state detail for Pro users.

articles.title: 183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know
articles.headline: 183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know
articles.description: What people usually mean by the 183-day tax residency rule, why it is common but not universal, and where day counting stops being the whole answer.
articles.intro: A practical explainer of what the 183-day rule usually means, where it is often oversimplified, and why day counts alone are not always the whole residency test.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Explains
section_contexts.summary: This page explains the general idea behind the 183-day tax residency rule for people who need a reliable mental model before they trust a spreadsheet, a relocation plan, or casual online advice.
detail_points: It is not tax advice, and it does not tell you whether you are resident in any specific country. That depends on the jurisdiction, the relevant year, the treaty position, and the rest of your facts.

The most important boundary: the 183-day rule is a common residency threshold, not a universal global rule. Some countries use it directly, some use it as one test among several, and some use other frameworks entirely.
what people usually mean when they talk about a "183-day rule"
why that shorthand is common but not universal
where day counts matter and where other residency factors can matter too

section_contexts.heading: What the 183-Day Rule Usually Means
section_contexts.summary: In general conversation, the phrase usually means this: if you spend more than about half the relevant year in a country, that country may treat your presence as strong enough to trigger tax-residency consequences or other tax analysis.
detail_points: That shorthand is useful as a warning light. If your travel is getting close to 183 days in one place, you should stop treating your record casually. But the shorthand is still only a starting point.
The real legal question is not "is there one worldwide 183-day rule?" but rather "how does this country use day counts in its own tax system?"

section_contexts.heading: Why the Rule Is Often Oversimplified
section_contexts.summary: The practical takeaway: "183 days" is usually the point where you should stop thinking in rough estimates. It is not the point where a general internet article can tell you your tax position with confidence.
detail_points: The counting period is not universal. Some systems use the calendar year, while others use a tax year that starts on a different date.

183 days may be decisive in one country and only one factor in another. In the UK, 183 days in the relevant tax year is one automatic UK residence test. In Australia, the 183-day test is only one of several residency tests and can still be displaced by other facts.
Other domestic factors can matter alongside day counts. Spain, for example, also looks at where a person's main centre or base of economic interests is located.
The phrase also appears in treaty and employment contexts. A "183-day rule" in a tax treaty or employment-income discussion is not automatically the same thing as a domestic tax-residence test.
Some countries do not use a simple single-year 183-day test at all. The U.S. substantial presence test uses a weighted three-year formula rather than a simple one-country, one-year threshold.

section_contexts.heading: When Day Counts Matter, and When Other Factors Can Matter Too
section_contexts.summary: Day counts matter because tax systems often need an objective presence test. But day counts are not always the whole residency analysis.
detail_points: Depending on the country and the question being asked, authorities may also look at factors such as:

That is why a person can spend fewer than 183 days in a country and still have a tax-residency issue there, or spend more than 183 days and still need a country-specific analysis of exceptions, treaty rules, or competing residence claims.
where you have a home available to you
where your family or personal ties are centred
where your work, business, or economic interests are based

section_contexts.heading: Common Counting Mistakes and False Assumptions
section_contexts.summary: Assuming every country uses the calendar year. Some jurisdictions count against a tax year that does not run from January to December.
detail_points: Assuming under 183 days means "safe." In many systems, other residency factors can still matter even when you are below the headline threshold.
Assuming every "183-day rule" means residency. Treaty and employment-income rules can use the same number for a different purpose.
Ignoring country-specific day-counting rules. Partial days, arrival and departure treatment, commuting days, or sporadic absences can matter depending on the jurisdiction.
Mixing immigration logic with tax logic. Visa limits and tax-residency tests are separate systems, even if both require careful travel records.
Waiting too long to clean up your travel record. Once the year is split across several countries, fixing dates from memory becomes harder than most people expect.

section_contexts.heading: Practical Caution and Professional-Advice Boundary
section_contexts.summary: This page is a general explainer, not a substitute for country-specific tax advice.
detail_points: Examples of current official sources that show this variation include HMRC's Statutory Residence Test guidance , the Australian Taxation Office's residency guidance , Spain's Tax Agency guidance on residence in Spain , the IRS substantial presence test , and the CRA's deemed-resident guidance .
Rules vary by jurisdiction, by relevant year, and sometimes by the exact type of income or residence question involved.
Domestic tax law and tax treaties can point to different tests or different next questions.
The same person can face possible residence claims in more than one country at once.
If the consequences matter, the next step is the relevant tax authority guidance and, where needed, qualified professional advice.

section_contexts.heading: When Manual Tracking Starts to Break Down
section_contexts.summary: Manual counting is manageable when you have one country, one obvious year, and a clean calendar. It becomes unreliable when you have:
detail_points: At that point, the problem is no longer just arithmetic. It is record quality. One missing weekend, one wrong arrival date, or one forgotten side trip can change the analysis you hand to an adviser.
multiple countries in the same year
different domestic counting periods to think about at once
uncertain arrival or departure dates from older trips
the need to reconstruct prior years for tax filings, treaty claims, or professional advice

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays Helps
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays is useful when you are past rough estimates and need a dated country-by-country travel record that can be reviewed repeatedly as questions come up.
detail_points: It does not decide your tax residency or replace professional advice. It gives you a cleaner factual record of where you were and when, which is usually the part that breaks first in spreadsheets. If you want the operational setup step inside the app, use Help Center: Trackers and Limits .

section_contexts.heading: Related articles

detail_points: US Substantial Presence Test: What Travelers Need to Know
What Counts as a 'Day' for Visa Purposes?
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)

section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays keeps a dated travel record by country so you do not have to rebuild the same residency timeline from memory every time a threshold question comes up.

articles.title: Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
articles.headline: Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
articles.description: How major country-counting systems differ, what they are actually trying to count, and why travel totals stop being comparable when the list is unclear.
articles.intro: A comparison of major country-counting systems for travelers who want a number that actually means something.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Explains
section_contexts.summary: This page explains why major country-counting systems disagree and what each one is actually trying to count.
detail_points: why there is no single authoritative travel list
what common systems such as UN, ISO, FIFA, the Olympics, TCC, and NomadMania are measuring
why two travelers can tell the truth and still report different totals
when country totals stop being comparable

section_contexts.heading: What a Country List Is Actually Trying to Count
section_contexts.summary: Different lists are built for different purposes. Some are about sovereignty. Some are about coding systems. Some are about sport representation. Some are built by travel communities that want a more granular map of meaningful destinations.
detail_points: That is why the right question is not “which list is correct?” It is “what is this list designed to count?”

section_contexts.heading: Why One Total Is Not Comparable Without the List
section_contexts.summary: A traveler counting Hong Kong separately, another folding it into China, and a third counting only UN states can all be internally consistent while reporting different totals. The problem is not dishonesty. The problem is that the counting frame is unstated.
detail_points: That is why “I’ve been to 80 countries” is not a fully meaningful claim unless the list is clear.
The practical rule: pick a system, use it consistently, and say which system it is when the total matters.

section_contexts.heading: What Makes List Comparisons Misleading
section_contexts.summary: Mixing systems opportunistically.
detail_points: Treating sovereignty, coding, sport representation, and travel distinctiveness as if they were the same thing.
Comparing totals without naming the denominator.

section_contexts.heading: Practical Boundary
section_contexts.summary: This page is about travel counting, not legal identity. Immigration, visa scope, and tax residence questions should be answered with the relevant legal framework, not with a collector list.

section_contexts.heading: When Country Counting Stops Being Simple
section_contexts.summary: Country counting stops being simple as soon as territories, disputed places, and subnational regions start to matter to you. At that point the problem is no longer “how many?” It becomes “by which system, and for what purpose?”

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays Helps
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays helps because it lets the same underlying travel record be viewed through different counting lenses instead of forcing one global list on every user. That makes the count more honest without pretending the world has one universally accepted collector denominator.

section_contexts.heading: Related articles
section_contexts.summary: How Many Countries Are There in the World?
detail_points: Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)

section_contexts.heading: Keep one record even when the counting system changes
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays lets the same travel history be viewed through different counting modes without rebuilding the record from scratch.

articles.title: Digital Nomad Visa Day Limits: A Country-by-Country Overview
articles.headline: Digital Nomad Visa Day Limits: A Country-by-Country Overview
articles.description: A practical overview of how common nomad destinations frame short stays, extensions, and longer remote-work routes, without pretending one summary can replace official programme rules.
articles.intro: A cautious nomad-focused overview of stay patterns across common destinations, with the emphasis on how these rules differ rather than on one-size-fits-all numbers.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Explains
section_contexts.summary: This page explains how to think about nomad stay limits across countries rather than pretending one roundup can settle every current visa rule.
detail_points: Most important caution: country-by-country nomad roundups are useful for orientation, not for final decisions. The exact route, nationality, documentation, and current programme version can change faster than a general article.
why “day limit” means different things in different destinations
how short-stay windows, per-entry stays, calendar-year caps, and remote-work routes differ
why country-by-country summaries age quickly
what questions matter before you treat a nomad stay rule as reliable

section_contexts.heading: The Four Patterns That Matter More Than the Country Names
section_contexts.summary: Once you see these patterns clearly, country-by-country differences become easier to classify and much harder to confuse.
detail_points: Rolling-window short stays — for example, the Schengen 90/180 model.
Per-entry visitor stays — a country may allow a certain number of days per entry, sometimes with extensions.
Calendar-year caps — a country may let you stay only up to a total amount within a year even if entries are separate.
Dedicated remote-work or nomad routes — these are not just longer tourist stays; they usually have their own eligibility and paperwork.

section_contexts.heading: Illustrative Country Patterns
section_contexts.summary: Examples often cited by nomads span categories more than fixed answers: the Schengen area for rolling-window short stays, some visa-free destinations for unusually long stays, and countries that distinguish ordinary visitor permission from longer remote-work or national-route options. The exact programme details still need to be checked against current official sources before you rely on them.

section_contexts.heading: Why Current Programme Details Need Rechecking
section_contexts.summary: The nomad landscape keeps shifting. Routes that launch in one year can become usable later, documentation requirements can change, and new remote-work options can create a separate national-permit question alongside ordinary visitor-day limits.
detail_points: That is why dated country-by-country roundups are risky. Treat any specific claim about income thresholds, bank-statement windows, renewal periods, family eligibility, or application channels as provisional until you confirm it on the official visa portal, immigration ministry, or consulate page for the route you plan to use.
Tracking this yourself? AtlasDays keeps a private, dated travel record on your iPhone and counts the days that matter — visa limits, residency thresholds, and country totals. Get the app →

section_contexts.heading: What Country-by-Country Roundups Usually Get Wrong
section_contexts.summary: They flatten route differences. A tourist entry, a visitor extension, and a remote-work permit are not the same thing.
detail_points: They ignore nationality dependence. A stay rule that is true for one passport may not be true for another.
They hide tax and residence consequences. A generous stay allowance does not mean the country is indifferent to longer physical presence.
They age quickly. Digital-nomad programmes are still evolving and often get renamed, tightened, or replaced.

section_contexts.heading: What to Verify Before You Rely on a Nomad Stay Rule
section_contexts.summary: Are you looking at a visitor route or a dedicated remote-work route?
detail_points: Is the limit per entry, per rolling window, or per calendar year?
Does the rule apply to your nationality?
Is remote work actually permitted on that route, or are you assuming it is tolerated?
What other threshold becomes relevant if you stay longer — especially tax or residence presence?

section_contexts.heading: Practical Caution and Official Boundary
section_contexts.summary: This page is a comparison framework, not a substitute for programme pages or current immigration guidance. For any real move or extended stay, the official route page and current government source should win over a general nomad summary every time.
detail_points: For Schengen-type short stays, start with the European Commission's short-stay calculator and its Schengen area overview . For any country-specific nomad or visitor route, use that country's current immigration ministry, consulate, or official visa portal rather than relying on a roundup.

section_contexts.heading: When Manual Tracking Starts to Break Down
section_contexts.summary: Nomads rarely have one clock running. They often have several overlapping clocks: a visitor limit in one country, a rolling-window rule in another, and a tax-presence question somewhere else. That is where informal note-taking stops being reliable, even if each individual rule looked simple when viewed alone.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays Helps
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays helps because it keeps the underlying travel chronology in one place and lets you review multiple rule types against that same record. It does not replace official country rules or tell you which visa route you qualify for, but it does make the pattern side of nomad planning much harder to lose track of. If you want the operational setup step inside the app, use Help Center: Trackers and Limits .

articles.title: Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
articles.headline: Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
articles.description: How to think about layovers for immigration, day counting, and personal travel lists without pretending those questions all have the same answer.
articles.intro: A practical distinction between legal entry, airside transit, and personal travel counting.

section_contexts.heading: What This Page Explains
section_contexts.summary: This page explains why “does a layover count?” is really more than one question and why the answer depends on whether you mean immigration, day counting, or your own travel list.
detail_points: how airside transit differs from formal entry
why immigration and travel-list counting are not the same problem
where the answer becomes subjective
how to stay consistent once you pick your rule

section_contexts.heading: The Immigration Question
section_contexts.summary: For immigration and day-counting purposes, the meaningful distinction is usually whether you formally entered the country or remained in transit.
detail_points: If you stayed airside and never crossed the border into the country in the immigration sense, that is generally a very different situation from clearing passport control and entering. That difference is what matters for visa-day logic, stay calculations, and later chronology questions.
Practical takeaway: if the question is legal presence, the real issue is not whether the plane landed. It is whether you were treated as having entered the country under the relevant border rules.

section_contexts.heading: The Travel-List Question
section_contexts.summary: For personal travel counting, the answer is not universal. Some travelers count only places where they meaningfully left the airport. Others count any formal entry. Some do not count a place unless they spent real time beyond the transport chain itself.
detail_points: That is not a flaw. It just means personal travel lists are value systems rather than legal frameworks.

section_contexts.heading: Three Common Standards People Use
section_contexts.summary: No one of these is universally correct. What matters is that you apply your own rule consistently instead of changing it case by case.
detail_points: Tracking this yourself? AtlasDays keeps a private, dated travel record on your iPhone and counts the days that matter — visa limits, residency thresholds, and country totals. Get the app →
Strict: it counts only if you left the airport or had a meaningful on-the-ground visit.
Moderate: it counts if you formally entered the country, even if the stay was brief.
Lenient: landing there is enough for the personal list.

section_contexts.heading: What Usually Causes Confusion
section_contexts.summary: Mixing legal entry with personal collecting rules.
detail_points: Assuming all layovers are the same. Airside transit and border-crossing layovers are different.
Using one answer for every context. A personal travel list, a visa-day counter, and a residency record do not need the same threshold.

section_contexts.heading: Practical Caution and Boundary
section_contexts.summary: This page is a framing guide, not a universal immigration rule. Transit treatment can vary by country, route, and the exact question being asked. If the issue matters for a visa, residency, or formal application, use the specific regime’s guidance rather than a general travel-list article.

section_contexts.heading: When Manual Memory Starts to Break Down
section_contexts.summary: Layovers become a record-keeping problem later, not during the trip. Years afterward, the difficult part is often remembering whether you actually entered, whether the stop was only airside, and whether that distinction matters for the question now being asked. That is why the distinction is worth recording even if your personal travel list rule is flexible.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays Helps
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays is useful because it lets you keep the chronology clear without forcing one personal travel-list philosophy on you. It separates transit from ordinary trip logic so the record stays reviewable later even if your own “visited” standard is more subjective. If you want the operational setup step inside the app, use Help Center: Trip Modes and Record Quality .

section_contexts.summary: What Counts as a "Day" for Visa Purposes?
detail_points: How Many Countries Are There in the World?
Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared

section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays helps you preserve the chronology so transit and actual entry do not blur together years afterward.
